enrichment.short_description: OpenOLAT is an open-source learning management system (LMS) built by frentix GmbH for universities, enterprises, and public institutions to deliver online courses, assessments, and certifications.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); hosted plans from frentix on request
enrichment.seo_title: OpenOLAT Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenOLAT review 2026: explore this open-source LMS's course authoring, testing engine, video tools, pricing, hosting options, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenOLAT (Open Online Learning And Training) is an open-source learning management system originally forked in 2011 from the University of Zurich's OLAT project by frentix GmbH, a Zurich, Switzerland-based software company founded in 2006 by Florian Gnaegi, one of OLAT's original creators. OpenOLAT is used by universities, vocational schools, enterprises, and public-sector organizations across German-speaking Europe and beyond to run online courses, blended learning programs, certifications, and large-scale examinations. Its core differentiator is a fully independent, high-stakes testing environment capable of running proctored, timed, randomized exams alongside self-assessment quizzes, which has made it popular with universities and professional certification bodies that need exam-grade reliability rather than a lightweight quiz plugin. Other standout capabilities include integrated BigBlueButton video conferencing, YouTube-style video hosting with embedded interactive questions, document collaboration via OnlyOffice, LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2 and Shibboleth single sign-on for institutional identity systems, and a course editor supporting SCORM, xAPI, and IMS QTI content. The interface and course content can be delivered in around 20 languages, reflecting adoption across multilingual European institutions. OpenOLAT is free to download, install, and self-host under its open-source license; organizations that prefer not to run their own servers can instead pay frentix for managed hosting, technical support, training, and customization, with pricing quoted individually per institution rather than published as fixed tiers. This dual model, a free open-source core plus optional paid hosting and services, mirrors other institution-grade open-source LMS platforms. Its closest competitors are Moodle, the dominant open-source LMS globally, plus commercial platforms like Canvas, Blackboard Learn, and ILIAS, another German-origin open-source LMS. Compared with Moodle, OpenOLAT is often described by IT teams as more lightweight to administer and faster out of the box, with a more opinionated, less plugin-dependent architecture, while Moodle has a much larger global plugin ecosystem. OpenOLAT's target audience is IT and e-learning teams at universities, corporate training departments, and government agencies that need a self-hostable, data-sovereign LMS, particularly organizations in Switzerland and Germany subject to strict data residency requirements. Because it can be fully self-hosted in the EU or Switzerland, it is frequently chosen by institutions that cannot use US-hosted SaaS LMS platforms for compliance reasons. frentix, the company behind OpenOLAT, remains a small team of roughly a dozen to a few dozen employees, funding development primarily through hosting and support contracts rather than venture capital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenOLAT is a web-based, open-source learning management system used for teaching, assessment, and communication at universities, vocational schools, enterprises, and government institutions. The name stands for Open Online Learning And Training, and the project was forked in 2011 from the University of Zurich's original OLAT platform by frentix GmbH, a Zurich-based company co-founded by one of OLAT's original developers.

Unlike many open-source LMS platforms that started as hobby or community projects, OpenOLAT was built from day one by a company with deep institutional e-learning experience, and that heritage shows in its exam-grade testing engine, enterprise identity integrations, and focus on scalability for large student and employee populations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenOLAT's testing environment is built to run both low-stakes self-assessments and high-stakes, timed, randomized examinations independently of the course-authoring tools, which is a common gap in lighter-weight LMS platforms. It integrates BigBlueButton for live video conferencing and webinars, supports YouTube-style video hosting with embedded interactive questions, and includes OnlyOffice-based document collaboration for group work.

On the administration side, OpenOLAT connects to LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, and Shibboleth for single sign-on, supports SCORM, xAPI, and IMS QTI content standards, and includes calendar, attendance tracking, and quality-management/evaluation tools suited to accredited education providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenOLAT itself is free and open source: any organization can download, install, and run it on its own servers at no licensing cost. For institutions that prefer not to manage their own infrastructure, frentix offers managed hosting ranging from full operational management to technical support only.

Because hosting and support needs vary widely by institution size and requirements, frentix does not publish fixed price tiers; pricing is quoted individually after a scoping conversation, similar to how most other institution-grade open-source LMS vendors operate.

features.title: Independent Testing and Assessment Engine
features.description: A dedicated exam engine supports timed, randomized, proctorable high-stakes tests separately from lightweight self-assessment quizzes.

features.title: Integrated Video Conferencing
features.description: Built-in BigBlueButton support enables live webinars and video classes directly inside a course.

features.title: SCORM, xAPI, and QTI Course Authoring
features.description: Courses can import and export standard e-learning content formats for interoperability with other systems.

features.title: Document Collaboration
features.description: OnlyOffice integration allows learners and instructors to co-edit documents inside a course workspace.

features.title: Enterprise Identity Integration
features.description: LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, and Shibboleth support institutional single sign-on at scale.

features.title: Multi-language Interface
features.description: The platform interface and course content can be delivered in roughly 20 languages.

features.title: Calendar and Attendance Tracking
features.description: Built-in scheduling tools handle instructor calendars, room booking, and attendance records.

features.title: Quality Management Tools
features.description: Integrated evaluation and survey tools support institutional accreditation and quality-assurance processes.

pros: Free and open source with full source code access
Built-in exam-grade testing environment suitable for high-stakes assessments
Strong data sovereignty for EU and Swiss institutions via self-hosting
Deep enterprise identity integration, including LDAP and SSO
Actively maintained with a long-running institutional user base
cons: Smaller plugin ecosystem than Moodle
Self-hosting requires dedicated IT and Java expertise
Documentation and community discussion skew toward German language
Managed hosting pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Interface feels more utilitarian than newer consumer-style LMS platforms

faqs.question: Is OpenOLAT free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenOLAT is open-source software that can be downloaded and self-hosted at no licensing cost. Organizations that want managed hosting or support pay frentix, the company behind the project, for those services.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenOLAT?
faqs.answer: OpenOLAT is developed and maintained by frentix GmbH, a Zurich, Switzerland-based company founded in 2006 by one of the original OLAT developers.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OLAT and OpenOLAT?
faqs.answer: OLAT was the original learning management system built by the University of Zurich. OpenOLAT is a fork created in 2011 by frentix after the university restructured its own development process.

faqs.question: Can OpenOLAT be used for high-stakes exams?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenOLAT includes an independent testing environment designed for timed, randomized, high-stakes examinations in addition to informal self-assessment quizzes.

faqs.question: Does OpenOLAT support SCORM content?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenOLAT supports SCORM, xAPI, and IMS QTI standards for importing and exporting course content.

faqs.question: How many languages does OpenOLAT support?
faqs.answer: The interface and course content can be delivered in roughly 20 languages, reflecting its use across multilingual European institutions.

faqs.question: How does OpenOLAT compare to Moodle?
faqs.answer: OpenOLAT is generally considered more lightweight to administer with a more opinionated architecture, while Moodle has a larger global plugin ecosystem and community.

use_cases.title: University online course delivery and exams
use_cases.description: Universities use OpenOLAT to run online and blended courses along with proctored, high-stakes examinations.

use_cases.title: Corporate training and compliance certification
use_cases.description: Enterprises use OpenOLAT to deliver employee training programs and track completion for compliance certification.

use_cases.title: Government and public-sector training programs
use_cases.description: Public institutions use OpenOLAT's self-hosting capability to run training programs while keeping data within national or EU borders.

enrichment.short_description: OpenOlitor is a free, open-source AGPL-licensed platform that helps Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) groups manage members, share planning, deliveries, invoicing, and communication.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); new features are community co-financed
enrichment.company_size: 10+ community contributors (no formal company; led by Tegonal GmbH, Bern)
enrichment.headquarters: Bern, Switzerland (community-run project with contributors across Switzerland, Germany, Austria, and Italy)
enrichment.seo_title: OpenOlitor Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenOlitor review 2026: how this open-source AGPL platform helps CSA groups manage members, deliveries, invoicing and shares, plus pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenOlitor is a free, open-source software platform purpose-built for Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) organizations, the farm-to-consumer model in which members subscribe to a share of a farm's harvest in advance. The project originated in Switzerland around 2014, conceived to give CSA groups, known as 'Solidarische Landwirtschaft' in German-speaking Europe, a shared, non-proprietary alternative to spreadsheets and closed commercial tools. Its first fully-financed version cost roughly CHF 154,000 and was co-funded by Switzerland's Federal Office for Agriculture (FOAG), the Bern-based software cooperative Tegonal GmbH, the CSA association Verein soliTerre, and a number of participating farms. OpenOlitor is not run by a conventional company; instead it is maintained by an international, loosely-organized team of more than ten contributors based in Switzerland, Germany, Austria, and Italy, operating under a 'Community Supported Software' funding model where the CSAs that use the platform help finance new features they need, mirroring the CSA farming model itself. The software was released under the GPL v3 license until April 2018 and the AGPL v3 license since, keeping the full source code freely available on GitHub. Functionally, OpenOlitor covers the operational backbone of running a CSA: member and household management, harvest-share and subscription planning, delivery-point and route scheduling, absence and vacation handling, automated invoicing, and bulk email communication with members, all accessible through a multilingual web interface translated into eight languages. It is actively used by around fifteen CSA organizations across Switzerland and Germany, making it a niche but purpose-fit tool rather than a broad commercial product. There is no traditional subscription pricing: the software itself is free to self-host, though CSAs typically need technical help or a hosting arrangement, often coordinated through Tegonal GmbH or community volunteers, to deploy and maintain an instance, and larger feature requests are typically crowd-funded by the participating CSA groups. OpenOlitor's competitors are less other CSA-specific platforms and more generic farm-share or subscription-box management tools and manual spreadsheet or email workflows; within the CSA space specifically it is one of very few dedicated open-source options. Its target audience is small to mid-size CSA farms and food cooperatives, primarily in German- and French-speaking Europe, run by farmers or volunteer coordinators who value data ownership, low cost, and a tool designed specifically around share-based agriculture rather than generic e-commerce or CRM software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenOlitor is an open-source administration platform built specifically for Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) groups, where consumers subscribe in advance to a share of a farm's harvest. The project has been developed since around 2014 by an international, informally-organized team based in Switzerland, Germany, Austria, and Italy, with early funding co-provided by Switzerland's Federal Office for Agriculture and the Bern-based software cooperative Tegonal GmbH.

Rather than being sold by a company, OpenOlitor is sustained through a 'Community Supported Software' model: the CSA organizations that rely on the tool contribute financially and organizationally to fund ongoing development, echoing the same solidarity-based funding logic used in the CSA farming model itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenOlitor handles the day-to-day operations of running a share-based farm: member and household records, harvest-share and subscription planning, delivery-point and route scheduling, absence and vacation handling, automated invoicing, and bulk email communication with members.

The platform is translated into eight languages and released under the AGPL v3 open-source license, giving CSA groups full access to the source code and the ability to self-host their data rather than depend on a proprietary vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenOlitor has no traditional pricing plans. The software is free to download and self-host under its open-source license, and there is no company charging subscription fees.

CSA groups that adopt it typically need to arrange their own hosting, either self-managed or through community volunteers and Tegonal GmbH, and larger new features are generally funded collectively by the participating CSA organizations rather than purchased individually.

features.title: Member and Household Management
features.description: Tracks CSA member records, households, and contact information in one system.

features.title: Share and Subscription Planning
features.description: Manages harvest-share sizes, subscription types, and seasonal planning for members.

features.title: Delivery Point and Route Scheduling
features.description: Coordinates delivery locations, pickup points, and distribution schedules.

features.title: Automated Invoicing
features.description: Generates and tracks invoices for member shares and subscriptions.

features.title: Absence and Vacation Management
features.description: Lets members register absences so deliveries and shares can be adjusted accordingly.

features.title: Bulk Email Communication
features.description: Sends group emails and updates to members directly from the platform.

features.title: Multilingual Interface
features.description: Available in eight languages to support CSA groups across several European countries.

pros: Completely free and open source under the AGPL v3 license
Purpose-built specifically for CSA and share-farming workflows
Community co-financing model keeps development aligned with real farm needs
Full data ownership through self-hosting
Actively used by CSA groups across multiple European countries
cons: No formal company means limited dedicated commercial support
Requires technical setup or community help to host and maintain
Small user base of roughly fifteen known CSAs limits the surrounding ecosystem
Documentation is primarily in German
Feature development pace depends on community co-financing availability

faqs.question: What is OpenOlitor used for?
faqs.answer: OpenOlitor is used by Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) groups to manage members, plan harvest shares, schedule deliveries, and handle invoicing and communication.

faqs.question: Is OpenOlitor free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenOlitor is free, open-source software released under the AGPL v3 license. There is no vendor charging subscription fees, though hosting and support may involve community-arranged costs.

faqs.question: Who develops OpenOlitor?
faqs.answer: OpenOlitor is maintained by an international, informally-organized team of more than ten contributors based in Switzerland, Germany, Austria, and Italy, with support from the Bern-based cooperative Tegonal GmbH.

faqs.question: What license is OpenOlitor released under?
faqs.answer: OpenOlitor was released under GPL v3 until April 2018 and has been released under AGPL v3 since then.

faqs.question: How many organizations use OpenOlitor?
faqs.answer: OpenOlitor is used by roughly fifteen CSA organizations across Switzerland and Germany.

faqs.question: What languages is OpenOlitor available in?
faqs.answer: OpenOlitor is translated into eight languages, including German, French, Italian, and English.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to run OpenOlitor?
faqs.answer: Yes, some technical knowledge or community assistance is typically needed to deploy and host an OpenOlitor instance, since there is no commercial vendor offering turnkey setup.

use_cases.title: CSA farm managing weekly harvest shares
use_cases.description: A farm uses OpenOlitor to plan harvest shares, schedule deliveries, and invoice members each season.

use_cases.title: Food cooperative coordinating deliveries
use_cases.description: A food cooperative uses OpenOlitor to manage multiple delivery points and member communication.

use_cases.title: Volunteer-run share network
use_cases.description: A volunteer-run share network uses OpenOlitor to track member absences and adjust deliveries accordingly.

enrichment.short_description: OpenPhone is a modern, VoIP-based business phone system for startups and small teams, offering shared phone numbers, calling, texting, and AI-powered call features; the company rebranded to Quo in late 2025.
enrichment.seo_title: OpenPhone Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenPhone (now Quo) review 2026: business VoIP phone features, pricing plans, AI call tools, pros, cons, FAQs and the best alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenPhone is a cloud-based business phone system founded in 2018 by Daryna Kulya and Mahyar Raissi to replace traditional desk phones and consumer apps with a modern, collaborative VoIP platform built for startups and small-to-midsize teams. Headquartered in San Francisco, the company has raised roughly $105 million across five funding rounds from investors including Tiger Global Management, Slow Ventures, and Craft Ventures, with its most recent round closing in September 2025. In late 2025 the company rebranded from OpenPhone to Quo (quo.com), positioning the product around AI-powered business communication while keeping the same core phone-system technology; the two names refer to the same underlying company and product line. OpenPhone lets teams create shared business phone numbers that multiple people can call, text, and manage from one shared inbox, similar to how a team shares a group email inbox, which differentiates it from single-user consumer VoIP apps and from legacy PBX hardware systems. Key capabilities include unlimited calling and messaging in the US and Canada, voicemail transcription, an AI agent (branded Sona) that can answer routine calls, AI-generated call summaries and transcripts, call transfers and group calling, auto-attendant phone menus, automatic call recording, and native integrations with HubSpot and Salesforce for sales and support teams. Pricing is per-user and tiered: Starter at $15/user/month (annual) or $19/month-to-month for basic calling and texting; Business at $23/user/month (annual) or $33/month-to-month adding AI summaries, integrations, and phone menus; and Scale at $35/user/month (annual) or $47/month-to-month adding AI call tagging and dedicated onboarding. All plans include a 7-day free trial with no credit card required. OpenPhone's target audience is small business owners, sales teams, and customer support teams who need a professional multi-line phone system without provisioning traditional telecom hardware, and who value modern collaboration features like shared numbers and AI transcription over legacy PBX feature depth. Its main competitors include RingCentral, Dialpad, Grasshopper, Google Voice for Business, Aircall, and Nextiva, with OpenPhone generally positioned as the more startup-friendly, design-forward, and AI-native option compared to more enterprise-heavy legacy VoIP providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenPhone is a cloud-based business phone system founded in 2018 by Daryna Kulya and Mahyar Raissi and headquartered in San Francisco. It was built as a modern replacement for legacy desk phones and PBX systems, letting small teams set up business phone numbers that work through an app rather than physical hardware.

In late 2025 the company rebranded to Quo, expanding its focus toward AI-powered business communication while retaining the same underlying phone-system product. The two names describe the same company and platform, with existing OpenPhone customers migrated to the Quo brand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's signature feature is the shared phone number and shared inbox, which lets multiple team members call, text, and see conversation history from one business line, much like a shared team email inbox. It also includes AI call summaries and transcripts, an AI voice agent (Sona) that can handle routine incoming calls, and automatic call recording.

Additional features include auto-attendant phone menus, group calling and call transfers, voicemail transcription, and native integrations with HubSpot and Salesforce, making it a common choice for sales and customer support teams that need calling built into their CRM workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenPhone uses per-user, tiered subscription pricing with a discount for annual billing. Starter costs $15/user/month billed annually (or $19/user/month billed monthly) and covers core calling, texting, and voicemail transcription.

Business costs $23/user/month annually (or $33 monthly) and adds AI call summaries, CRM integrations, and phone menus, while Scale costs $35/user/month annually (or $47 monthly) and adds AI call tagging and dedicated onboarding support. All plans include a 7-day free trial with no credit card required.

features.title: Shared Business Phone Numbers
features.description: Multiple team members can call, text, and manage conversations from one shared business number and inbox.

features.title: AI Call Summaries and Transcripts
features.description: Calls are automatically transcribed and summarized for quick review without replaying the full recording.

features.title: Sona AI Agent
features.description: An AI voice agent can answer routine incoming calls and handle basic requests automatically.

features.title: Unlimited US and Canada Calling and Texting
features.description: All plans include unlimited domestic calling and messaging within the US and Canada.

features.title: HubSpot and Salesforce Integrations
features.description: Native CRM integrations sync call and text activity directly into sales and support workflows.

features.title: Auto-Attendant Phone Menus
features.description: Configurable phone menus route incoming calls to the right team member or department.

features.title: Automatic Call Recording
features.description: Calls are recorded automatically on Business and Scale plans for training and quality review.

features.title: Group Calling and Call Transfers
features.description: Teams can ring multiple people at once or transfer an active call to a colleague.

pros: Modern shared-inbox model makes team texting and calling collaborative
Strong AI features, including summaries, transcripts, and an AI agent, built into core plans
Fast setup with no phone hardware required
Native CRM integrations with HubSpot and Salesforce
Transparent per-user pricing with a free trial
cons: Recent rebrand to Quo has caused some confusion among existing customers
Higher-tier AI features require the Business or Scale plan
International calling coverage is more limited than legacy telecom providers
No permanent free plan
Advanced call-center features trail dedicated contact-center software

faqs.question: Is OpenPhone still called OpenPhone?
faqs.answer: The company rebranded from OpenPhone to Quo in late 2025. It is the same company and product, now marketed under the Quo name at quo.com.

faqs.question: How much does OpenPhone cost?
faqs.answer: Plans range from $15/user/month (Starter, billed annually) up to $35/user/month (Scale, billed annually), with higher month-to-month pricing available on all tiers.

faqs.question: Can multiple people share one OpenPhone number?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenPhone's core feature is a shared business number and shared inbox that multiple team members can use together.

faqs.question: Does OpenPhone offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, all plans include a 7-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: What integrations does OpenPhone support?
faqs.answer: OpenPhone integrates natively with HubSpot and Salesforce, among other tools, for sales and support teams.

faqs.question: Who founded OpenPhone?
faqs.answer: OpenPhone was founded in 2018 by Daryna Kulya and Mahyar Raissi and is headquartered in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Who is OpenPhone best for?
faqs.answer: OpenPhone is best suited to small businesses, startups, and sales or support teams that want a modern, collaborative phone system without traditional PBX hardware.

enrichment.short_description: OpenProject is an open-source, web-based project management platform offering Gantt charts, agile and Scrum boards, time tracking, and BIM tools for teams that need self-hostable or EU-hosted project management software.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community edition); paid plans from about EUR 5.95/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: OpenProject Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenProject review 2026: open-source project management features, Gantt charts, agile boards, pricing tiers, pros, cons and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenProject is an open-source project management platform run by OpenProject GmbH, headquartered in Berlin, Germany. The project traces back to 2012, when it forked from the older open-source tool ChiliProject, and has since grown into one of the most widely deployed self-hostable project management systems, used by government agencies, universities, and enterprises that require full control over their data, particularly in the EU and other regions with strict data-residency requirements. OpenProject's differentiator versus SaaS-only competitors like Asana, Monday.com, and ClickUp is that it can be run entirely on-premises or in a customer's own cloud, alongside a hosted cloud edition, giving IT and public-sector buyers a compliant middle ground between free open-source tools and closed enterprise SaaS. The platform bundles interactive Gantt chart scheduling, agile and Scrum or Kanban boards, backlogs and sprint planning, time and cost tracking, a wiki, meeting management, budgeting, and native Building Information Modeling (BIM) support for construction and engineering teams, a feature set that goes well beyond a typical open-source project tracker. OpenProject uses a tiered pricing model: the Community edition is free and open source (GPLv3) for unlimited self-hosted use with community support; paid tiers layer on enterprise add-ons and support, starting with Basic at roughly EUR 5.95 per user per month (25-user minimum), Professional at about EUR 10.95 per user per month, Premium at about EUR 15.95 per user per month (100-user minimum, includes remote-hands support), and a custom-quoted Corporate tier for organizations of 250-plus users needing dedicated support engineers and integration with Germany's openDesk government software suite. BIM functionality can be added to any paid plan for roughly EUR 1 per user per month. OpenProject GmbH employs a small team, generally reported in the 30-50 employee range, and has grown primarily through its open-source community and government or enterprise services contracts rather than large venture funding rounds. Its core audience spans software teams practicing agile development, engineering and construction firms needing BIM-integrated scheduling, and public-sector or regulated organizations that cannot use US-hosted SaaS project tools for compliance reasons. Competing products include Jira and Asana in the broader project management category, Redmine among open-source peers, and BIM-focused tools like Autodesk BIM 360 for the construction-specific use case, with OpenProject's open-source self-hosting option remaining its clearest differentiator against nearly all mainstream competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenProject is an open-source project management platform headquartered in Berlin, Germany, tracing back to a 2012 fork of the earlier ChiliProject tool. It has grown into one of the most widely deployed self-hostable project management systems, particularly popular with government agencies, universities, and EU-based enterprises that need to keep project data on their own infrastructure.

Unlike SaaS-only competitors, OpenProject can run entirely on-premises, in a customer's own cloud, or on OpenProject's hosted cloud, giving IT and public-sector buyers a compliant middle ground between free open-source tools and closed commercial SaaS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenProject combines classic project planning with agile workflows: interactive Gantt chart scheduling sits alongside Scrum and Kanban boards, backlog and sprint planning, and roadmap views, letting teams mix waterfall and agile methods in the same workspace.

It also includes time and cost tracking, a wiki, meeting management, budgeting tools, and native Building Information Modeling (BIM) support, an uncommon feature among general-purpose project management tools that makes it popular with construction and engineering teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community edition is free, open source under GPLv3, and can be self-hosted with no user limit, though support is community-based. Paid cloud or on-premises plans start with Basic at about EUR 5.95/user/month, Professional at about EUR 10.95/user/month, and Premium at about EUR 15.95/user/month, each requiring minimum user counts of 25 to 100.

A custom-quoted Corporate tier is available for organizations with 250 or more users, adding a dedicated support engineer and integration with Germany's openDesk suite. BIM features can be added to any paid plan for roughly EUR 1 per user per month.

features.title: Interactive Gantt Chart Scheduling
features.description: Visual, drag-and-drop scheduling with dependencies for classic project timelines.

features.title: Agile and Scrum or Kanban Boards
features.description: Backlogs, sprint planning, and Kanban boards support agile software development workflows.

features.title: Time and Cost Tracking
features.description: Built-in time logging and budget tracking tied directly to project tasks.

features.title: BIM Integration
features.description: Native Building Information Modeling support for construction and engineering scheduling.

features.title: Wiki and Meeting Management
features.description: Project wikis and structured meeting agendas keep documentation and decisions centralized.

features.title: Budgeting and Reporting
features.description: Cost planning and reporting tools track budgets against project progress.

features.title: On-Premises or Cloud Hosting
features.description: Deployable fully on-premises, in a private cloud, or on OpenProject's hosted cloud.

features.title: Roadmap and Backlog Planning
features.description: Long-term roadmap views connect high-level plans to sprint-level backlogs.

pros: Free, fully-featured open-source Community edition
Can be fully self-hosted for data sovereignty and compliance
Native BIM support unusual among mainstream project management tools
Strong fit for government and EU-regulated organizations
Combines classic Gantt planning with agile Scrum and Kanban in one tool
cons: Interface feels less polished than modern SaaS competitors like Asana or Monday.com
Paid tiers require a 25-user minimum
Premium and Corporate tiers require large minimum user counts of 100 to 250
Self-hosting requires IT resources to maintain
Smaller third-party integration ecosystem than Jira

faqs.question: Is OpenProject really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community edition is free and open source under GPLv3 and can be self-hosted with no user limit, though support is community-based rather than commercial.

faqs.question: Can OpenProject be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenProject can run fully on-premises, in a private cloud, or on OpenProject's own hosted cloud service.

faqs.question: Does OpenProject support BIM?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenProject includes native Building Information Modeling support, which can be added to any paid plan for roughly EUR 1 per user per month.

faqs.question: What is the minimum number of users for paid plans?
faqs.answer: Basic and Professional plans require a 25-user minimum, Premium requires 100 users, and Corporate requires 250 users.

faqs.question: How does OpenProject compare to Jira?
faqs.answer: OpenProject offers a free, self-hostable open-source option that Jira does not, but has a smaller third-party integration ecosystem than Jira.

faqs.question: Who develops OpenProject?
faqs.answer: OpenProject is developed by OpenProject GmbH, headquartered in Berlin, Germany.

faqs.question: Does OpenProject offer a free trial for paid plans?
faqs.answer: OpenProject offers trial access to its paid cloud plans; exact trial terms should be confirmed on openproject.org since they can change.

use_cases.title: Software team running agile sprints

enrichment.short_description: OpenReader is a free, open-source, self-hostable read-along document reader that turns EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown files into synchronized text-to-speech audio with word-level highlighting and audiobook export.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); usage costs depend on the chosen text-to-speech provider
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (independent open-source project, no registered company)
enrichment.seo_title: OpenReader Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenReader review 2026: this open-source read-along document reader's TTS features, formats supported, self-hosting, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenReader is a free, open-source read-along document reader created by independent developer Richard Roberson (GitHub handle richardr1126) and first published in January 2025. It is a self-hosted web application, not a company or funded startup, distributed under the MIT license from its GitHub repository (richardr1126/openreader) and demoed publicly at openreader.richardr.dev. The tool converts EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown documents into a synchronized read-along experience: it plays high-quality text-to-speech narration while highlighting the exact word being spoken in the original document layout, using ONNX Whisper-based alignment to keep highlighting accurate even in dense, multi-column PDFs. Users can choose between hosted text-to-speech providers, including OpenAI, Replicate, and DeepInfra, for convenience, or fully self-hosted, offline TTS engines such as Kokoro, KittenTTS, and Orpheus for privacy and zero per-use cost, giving it flexibility that few commercial read-aloud apps offer. The application also supports full audiobook export, letting users generate a downloadable narrated audio file from any supported document. It is designed to be deployed via Docker on standard amd64 or arm64 servers, storing files on SeaweedFS or any S3-compatible bucket and data in SQLite or Postgres, with built-in authentication and automatic database migrations on startup. As a solo-developer open-source project rather than a commercial product, OpenReader has no pricing plans, sales team, or service-level agreement; its several hundred GitHub stars and active commit history indicate a genuinely maintained, actively developed project rather than an abandoned repository. It is best suited to privacy-conscious individuals, accessibility advocates, and self-hosting enthusiasts who want a narrated, synchronized reading experience for their personal EPUB or PDF library without depending on a subscription audiobook or read-aloud service such as Speechify, NaturalReader, or Voice Dream Reader, and who are comfortable running their own Docker-based server or paying directly for their own text-to-speech API usage instead of a bundled subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenReader is an open-source, self-hosted read-along document reader created by independent developer Richard Roberson and first published on GitHub in January 2025. It is not a company or commercial product; it is a solo-maintained open-source project released under the MIT license, with a public demo hosted at openreader.richardr.dev.

The tool is designed for people who want a synchronized narration experience, audio read aloud with word-level highlighting, for their own documents, without depending on a subscription audiobook service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenReader supports EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown files, using layout-aware parsing and ONNX Whisper-based alignment so that highlighting follows the true reading order even in complex, multi-column PDFs. Users can generate a full audiobook export from any supported document.

Text-to-speech can be powered by hosted providers such as OpenAI, Replicate, or DeepInfra, or by fully self-hosted, offline engines like Kokoro, KittenTTS, and Orpheus, giving users control over cost and data privacy. Deployment is via Docker, with support for S3-compatible storage and SQLite or Postgres databases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenReader itself is completely free and open source under the MIT license; there is no subscription or license fee for the software. Costs, if any, come only from the text-to-speech provider a user chooses to connect, such as per-request API charges from OpenAI, Replicate, or DeepInfra.
Users who self-host an offline TTS engine like Kokoro can run OpenReader with no ongoing usage costs at all, aside from their own server hosting expenses.

features.title: Audiobook Export
features.description: Generates a downloadable narrated audio file from any supported document.

features.title: Self-Hostable Docker Deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker on amd64 or arm64 servers with S3-compatible storage.

features.title: Built-in Authentication and Migrations
features.description: Includes built-in user authentication and automatic database migrations on startup.

Supports both hosted and fully self-hosted, offline TTS engines
Handles multiple document formats in one tool
Active GitHub project with hundreds of stars and ongoing updates
Docker-based deployment is straightforward for self-hosters
cons: Requires users to bring their own API keys or run their own TTS server for best quality
Maintained by an individual developer rather than a company, so support is community-based
No official cloud-hosted version with a support agreement
Setup requires some technical comfort with Docker, storage, and databases
Feature set and stability may change quickly as an early-stage independent project

faqs.answer: OpenReader supports EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown documents.

faqs.answer: Yes, OpenReader can be configured with self-hosted, offline text-to-speech engines like Kokoro, KittenTTS, or Orpheus.

faqs.answer: Yes, OpenReader can generate a full downloadable audiobook export from any supported document.

enrichment.short_description: OpenRemote is a 100% open-source IoT platform for asset management, automation, and data visualization, used by integrators and enterprises to build smart building, energy, and industrial IoT solutions.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); managed hosting and professional services quoted on request
enrichment.seo_title: OpenRemote Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenRemote review 2026: open-source IoT platform features, asset management, automation, pricing model, pros, cons and alternatives for integrators.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenRemote is a 100% open-source Internet of Things (IoT) platform originally founded in 2008 by Marc Fleury, the creator of the JBoss open-source Java application server, initially as a smart-home automation project. Around 2016 the company pivoted away from consumer smart-home products to focus on enterprise and OEM IoT device management, and it now operates as OpenRemote B.V., headquartered in Eindhoven, the Netherlands, with a US entity as well, remaining privately held and bootstrapped without external venture funding, financed instead by client contracts for customization, hosting, and support. The platform's core differentiator is that its entire stack, backend, rules engine, and management console, is released as genuinely open-source software on GitHub with no proprietary 'enterprise-only' fork, which sets it apart from most commercial IoT platforms that gate advanced features behind closed licenses. OpenRemote is built around an asset-centric data model: users define custom asset types, such as a building, a sensor, a vehicle, or a solar inverter, and track their attributes over time, with built-in protocol support for HTTP, MQTT, ModBus, KNX, Bluetooth, LoRaWAN, SNMP, Velbus, TCP, UDP, and serial connections, making it well-suited to bridging heterogeneous hardware in smart buildings, energy management, industrial IoT, fleet telematics, smart agriculture, and smart city deployments. Automation is handled through simple When-Then rules, a visual Flow Editor for data-processing pipelines, or Groovy scripting for complex logic, and every asset and attribute is automatically exposed through a unified REST API and WebSockets for integration with external systems. The platform includes a built-in dashboard builder for live and historical data visualization, map-based geofencing and location tracking, and multi-tenant realm isolation with enterprise-grade identity management powered by Keycloak (OAuth2/OpenID Connect). Because the software itself is free to download and self-host with no per-device or per-user licensing fees, OpenRemote's business model is services-based: the company earns revenue by providing professional configuration, custom integration development, and managed cloud hosting to system integrators and enterprises that would rather pay for expertise than build in-house. Its target audience is professional IoT integrators, OEMs, and enterprise facilities or energy teams managing complex, multi-protocol asset ecosystems, distinguishing it from consumer smart-home apps and positioning it closer to ThingsBoard and commercial platforms like Losant or Ubidots as points of comparison.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenRemote is a 100% open-source IoT platform originally founded in 2008 by Marc Fleury, the creator of the JBoss Java application server, initially as a smart-home automation project. Around 2016 the project pivoted to focus on enterprise and OEM IoT device management rather than consumer smart homes.

The company, OpenRemote B.V., is headquartered in Eindhoven, the Netherlands, and remains privately held and bootstrapped, funding development through client contracts for hosting, customization, and professional services rather than venture capital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenRemote uses an asset-centric data model where users define custom asset types, buildings, sensors, vehicles, or energy devices, and track their attributes over time. It includes native support for a wide range of IoT protocols, including MQTT, ModBus, KNX, Bluetooth, LoRaWAN, SNMP, and Velbus.

Automation can be configured with simple When-Then rules, a visual Flow Editor for data pipelines, or Groovy scripting for advanced logic. Every asset is automatically exposed via a unified REST API and WebSockets, and the platform includes a built-in dashboard builder, map-based geofencing, and Keycloak-powered enterprise identity management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenRemote's core platform is free and open source with no licensing fees for self-hosted use; the full stack is available on GitHub with no paywalled enterprise edition.

Organizations that prefer not to run and maintain their own infrastructure can pay OpenRemote for managed cloud hosting, custom configuration, and integration development, with pricing quoted individually per project rather than published as fixed tiers.

features.description: Enterprise-grade OAuth2 and OpenID Connect authentication built in.

pros: Fully open source with no paywalled enterprise features
Broad native protocol support reduces custom integration work
No per-device licensing fees for self-hosted deployments
Flexible automation options, from simple rules to full scripting
Bootstrapped, stable company focused on long-term open-source commitment
cons: No published SaaS pricing; managed hosting and support are quoted per project
Requires self-hosting or a paid managed instance to run in production
Smaller company means slower feature velocity than venture-funded IoT platforms
Steeper learning curve for teams new to asset-based IoT modeling
Smaller community and ecosystem than some larger IoT platforms

enrichment.short_description: OpenReplay is an open-source, self-hostable session replay and product analytics platform that lets developers record and replay user sessions, debug errors, and analyze product usage without sending data to a third party.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source, self-hosted); managed Dedicated plan from $199/month
enrichment.seo_title: OpenReplay Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenReplay review 2026: open-source session replay features, self-hosting, pricing plans, pros, cons, FAQs and top alternatives like LogRocket and Hotjar.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenReplay is an open-source session replay and product analytics platform built by Asayer, Inc., a company founded in 2018 by CEO Mehdi Osman and backed by Y Combinator's S19 batch. The company originally launched as Asayer, a closed-source SaaS session-replay tool, before pivoting the entire product to an open-source model in 2020 and rebranding as OpenReplay, a strategic shift aimed at differentiating from closed-source incumbents like FullStory, Hotjar, and LogRocket by letting engineering teams self-host the entire stack and keep user session data on their own infrastructure. Headquartered in Paris, France, the company has raised a modest $6 million-plus in funding from Y Combinator, Runa Capital, Expa, and Kima Ventures, positioning it as a lean, developer-focused alternative rather than a venture-scale growth story. OpenReplay's core product records everything a user does in a web or mobile application, clicks, scrolls, page navigation, network requests, console logs, JavaScript errors, and framework-level state such as Redux, Vuex, Pinia, and GraphQL queries, then reconstructs it as a pixel-accurate replay synchronized with over 40 performance metrics, letting engineering and product teams debug issues and understand user behavior side by side rather than relying on two separate tools. Additional capabilities include co-browsing for live customer support sessions, a lightweight bug and feedback capture tool called Spot, product analytics such as funnels, heatmaps, and user flows, and an MCP server for connecting session data to AI coding and debugging workflows. OpenReplay ships in three tiers: a fully free, self-hosted Open-Source edition where the customer manages their own infrastructure, scaling, and backups; a Dedicated managed-cloud tier starting around $199 per month, billed hourly at roughly $0.276 per hour, that runs in OpenReplay's cloud or the customer's own cloud (BYOC) with SSO/SAML and dedicated support; and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier for self-hosted deployments at scale with SAML/SCIM provisioning, audit logging, and multi-year contracts. This makes OpenReplay's core differentiator, versus LogRocket, FullStory, and Hotjar, its ability to be run entirely on a company's own servers for data-privacy or compliance reasons at zero licensing cost, which is particularly attractive to engineering-led teams, regulated industries, and companies outside the US that are wary of shipping user session data to a third-party SaaS vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenReplay is an open-source session replay and developer debugging platform built by Asayer, Inc., a Paris-based company founded in 2018 by CEO Mehdi Osman and backed by Y Combinator. The company originally operated as a closed-source SaaS tool named Asayer before pivoting the entire product to an open-source model in 2020 and rebranding as OpenReplay.

The pivot to open source was a deliberate strategy to differentiate from closed-source incumbents like FullStory, Hotjar, and LogRocket, letting engineering teams self-host the full stack and keep user session data entirely on their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenReplay records everything a user does in a web or mobile app, clicks, scrolls, network requests, console logs, JavaScript errors, and framework-level state (Redux, Vuex, Pinia, GraphQL), then reconstructs it as a pixel-accurate replay synchronized with more than 40 performance metrics, combining session replay and developer debugging in one tool.

It also includes co-browsing for live customer support sessions, a lightweight bug and feedback capture tool called Spot, product analytics such as funnels and heatmaps, and an MCP server for connecting session data into AI-assisted debugging workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Open-Source edition is completely free to self-host, with the customer responsible for their own infrastructure, scaling, and backups. The Dedicated tier is a managed cloud instance starting around $199 per month, billed hourly at roughly $0.276 per hour, and can run in OpenReplay's cloud or the customer's own cloud environment.
An Enterprise tier is available for large-scale self-hosted deployments, with custom pricing, SAML/SCIM provisioning, audit logging, and multi-year contracts.

features.description: Can run fully on customer infrastructure or in a bring-your-own-cloud setup.

pros: Fully open source and self-hostable at zero licensing cost
Combines session replay and developer debugging tools in one platform
Strong data-privacy story for regulated or security-conscious teams
Deep framework-level state capture beyond basic screen recording
Backed by Y Combinator with a focused, developer-first roadmap
cons: Self-hosting requires DevOps effort to run and scale the stack
Managed Dedicated plan pricing is higher than some SaaS competitors' entry tiers
Smaller company and community than LogRocket, Hotjar, or FullStory
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Product analytics features are less mature than dedicated analytics tools

faqs.question: Is OpenReplay really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Open-Source edition is free to self-host. Paid tiers exist for customers who want a managed cloud instance or enterprise-scale support.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OpenReplay and Asayer?
faqs.answer: Asayer was the original closed-source SaaS product; the company pivoted to an open-source model in 2020 and rebranded the product as OpenReplay.

faqs.question: Can OpenReplay be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting is the core of OpenReplay's Open-Source edition, and it is also supported in a bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) configuration on the Dedicated plan.

faqs.question: How does OpenReplay compare to LogRocket and Hotjar?
faqs.answer: OpenReplay's main differentiator is that it can be fully self-hosted at no licensing cost, while LogRocket and Hotjar are closed-source SaaS-only products.

faqs.question: What does the Dedicated plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Dedicated managed cloud plan starts around $199 per month, billed hourly at roughly $0.276 per hour.

faqs.question: Does OpenReplay support mobile apps?
faqs.answer: OpenReplay is primarily built for web applications; mobile session capture support should be confirmed against current documentation for specific frameworks.

faqs.question: Does OpenReplay capture Redux or GraphQL state?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenReplay captures framework-level state including Redux, Vuex, Pinia, and GraphQL queries alongside the session replay.

enrichment.short_description: OpenRouter is a unified API gateway that lets developers access 400-plus large language models from providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google through a single API with pay-as-you-go, usage-based pricing.
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; pay-as-you-go usage billed at provider cost plus a 5.5% platform fee
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: OpenRouter Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenRouter review 2026: unified LLM API features, 400+ model access, pay-as-you-go pricing, pros, cons, FAQs and alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenRouter is a unified API and routing layer for large language models, founded in 2023 by CEO Alex Atallah, previously co-founder and CEO of the NFT marketplace OpenSea, and headquartered in New York City. The company gives developers a single API endpoint and a single API key to access more than 400 AI models from over 70 providers, spanning OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta's Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek, and many others, removing the need to integrate each provider's SDK, billing, and rate limits separately. Its core value proposition is reliability and choice: OpenRouter automatically routes requests to the best-available provider for a given model, falls back to alternate providers during outages, and lets developers compare pricing, latency, and throughput across providers for the same model in real time, which has made it a default infrastructure layer for AI startups and hobbyist developers who want provider flexibility without vendor lock-in. OpenRouter raised roughly $40 million in early funding, a $12.5 million seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz and a $28 million Series A led by Menlo Ventures in April 2025, before raising a much larger $113 million Series B led by CapitalG, Alphabet's growth equity arm, in 2026, which valued the company at approximately $1.3 billion and made it an AI-infrastructure unicorn; Sacra estimated the company crossed roughly $50 million in annualized revenue in March 2026, up from about $19 million at the end of 2025. Pricing is primarily usage-based: OpenRouter offers a free tier with access to 25-plus free models and roughly 50 requests per day, a pay-as-you-go tier with no minimum spend where customers pay the underlying model's list price plus a 5.5% platform fee, with volume discounts available, and an Enterprise tier offering SSO, contractual SLAs, dedicated rate limits, and up to $200,000 of monthly inference at list price with no markup before the standard fee applies above that threshold. OpenRouter's target audience is AI application developers, from solo builders to enterprise engineering teams, who need to run, test, or fall back across multiple LLM providers without managing separate contracts and billing relationships with each one. Its closest competitors include direct provider APIs from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, other LLM gateway or routing tools such as Portkey and LiteLLM, and cloud-hosted model marketplaces like Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI, with OpenRouter differentiated by its breadth of models, transparent per-model pricing comparison, and simple unified billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenRouter is a unified API and routing layer for large language models, founded in 2023 by Alex Atallah, previously co-founder and CEO of the NFT marketplace OpenSea, and headquartered in New York City. It gives developers a single API endpoint and API key to access more than 400 AI models from over 70 providers, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Mistral, and DeepSeek.

The company has grown quickly, raising a $113 million Series B led by CapitalG, Alphabet's growth equity arm, in 2026 at an approximate $1.3 billion valuation, following an earlier $12.5 million seed round from Andreessen Horowitz and a $28 million Series A led by Menlo Ventures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenRouter automatically routes requests to the best-available provider for a chosen model and falls back to alternate providers during outages, improving reliability compared with calling a single provider directly. Developers can compare real-time pricing, latency, and throughput across providers offering the same model.

The platform gives access to 400-plus models through one integration and one API key, removing the need to manage separate SDKs, billing relationships, and rate limits with each individual AI provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenRouter offers a free tier with access to more than 25 free models and roughly 50 requests per day. Its main pricing model is pay-as-you-go, with no minimum spend, where customers pay the underlying model provider's list price plus a 5.5% platform fee.

An Enterprise tier adds SSO, contractual SLAs, dedicated rate limits, and volume-based fee discounts, including up to $200,000 of monthly inference at list price with no markup before the standard fee applies above that threshold.

faqs.answer: OpenRouter was founded in 2023 by Alex Atallah, previously co-founder and CEO of the NFT marketplace OpenSea.

faqs.question: How does OpenRouter compare to using OpenAI or Anthropic directly?
faqs.answer: OpenRouter adds a platform fee on top of direct provider pricing but offers automatic failover, unified billing, and access to many providers through one integration.

faqs.question: Is OpenRouter suitable for enterprise use?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenRouter offers an Enterprise tier with SSO, contractual SLAs, dedicated rate limits, and volume-based fee discounts.

use_cases.title: AI app developer needing multi-model flexibility
use_cases.description: A developer builds an app that can switch between AI models and providers without rewriting integration code.

use_cases.title: Enterprise needing provider redundancy and SLAs
use_cases.description: An enterprise engineering team uses OpenRouter for automatic failover and contractual reliability guarantees across providers.

use_cases.title: Researchers comparing model performance
use_cases.description: Researchers and hobbyists use OpenRouter to test and compare many models' outputs, cost, and latency in one place.

features: Credit card and crypto payment options
